content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 16 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 108v–108r | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_016_page_001.jpg, DEU_016_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 153–154

---

1 Observe the month of Abib and keep the Passover to the LORD your God, for in the month of Abib the LORD your God brought you out of Egypt by night.

2 And you shall sacrifice the Passover offering to the LORD your God — from the flock or the herd — in the place that the LORD will choose, to make his name dwell there.

3 You shall eat no leavened bread with it. For seven days you shall eat it with unleavened bread — the bread of affliction — for you came out of the land of Egypt in haste, so that all the days of your life you may remember the day you came out of the land of Egypt.

4 No leaven shall be seen with you in all your territory for seven days, and none of the flesh that you sacrifice on the evening of the first day shall remain until morning.

5 You may not offer the Passover sacrifice within any of your gates that the LORD your God is giving you,

6 but at the place that the LORD your God will choose, to make his name dwell — there you shall sacrifice the Passover, in the evening at the going down of the sun, at the season when you came out of Egypt.

7 And you shall cook it and eat it in the place that the LORD your God will choose, and in the morning you shall turn and go back to your tents.

8 For six days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there shall be a solemn assembly to the LORD your God; you shall do no work.

9 You shall count seven weeks. From the time the sickle is first put to the standing grain you shall begin to count seven weeks.

10 Then you shall keep the Feast of Weeks to the LORD your God with the tribute of a freewill offering from your hand, which you shall give as the LORD your God blesses you.

11 And you shall rejoice before the LORD your God — you and your son and your daughter, your male servant and your female servant, the Levite who is within your gates, and the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow who are among you — in the place that the LORD your God will choose, to make his name dwell there.

12 You shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt; and you shall be careful to observe these statutes.

13 You shall keep the Feast of Booths for seven days, when you have gathered in from your threshing floor and from your winepress.

14 You shall rejoice in your feast — you and your son and your daughter, your male servant and your female servant, the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow who are within your gates.

15 For seven days you shall keep the feast to the LORD your God in the place that the LORD will choose, because the LORD your God will bless you in all your produce and in all the work of your hands, so that you will be altogether joyful.

16 Three times a year all your males shall appear before the LORD your God in the place that he will choose: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread, at the Feast of Weeks, and at the Feast of Booths. They shall not appear before the LORD empty-handed.

17 Every man shall give as he is able, according to the blessing of the LORD your God that he has given you.

18 You shall appoint judges and officers in all your gates, which the LORD your God is giving you, according to your tribes, and they shall judge the people with righteous judgment.

19 You shall not pervert justice; you shall not show partiality; and you shall not accept a bribe, for the bribe blinds the eyes of the wise and perverts the words of the righteous.

20 Justice, justice you shall pursue, so that you may live and possess the land that the LORD your God is giving you.

21 You shall not plant any tree as an Asherah beside the altar of the LORD your God that you shall make.

22 And you shall not set up a pillar — which the LORD your God hates.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 1 — chodesh ha-aviv (month of Abib):** "Month of Abib" retained as the pre-exilic calendar name for the first spring month (later called Nisan). *Aviv* means "young ears of grain" — the barley harvest month. LXX: *ton menon ton neon* ("the month of new grain") — a functional rather than nominal rendering.

**Verse 2 — pesach (Passover sacrifice):** "Passover offering" for *pesach* — the sacrificial animal of the festival, by extension naming the feast itself. Deuteronomy notably expands Passover to include cattle (*bakar*) as well as small livestock (*tzon*), in contrast to the Exodus 12 lamb. LXX: *thysiaseis to pascha* — borrowing the Hebrew word as a loanword into Greek.

**Verse 3 — lechem oni (bread of affliction):** "Bread of affliction" for *lechem oni* — the unleavened bread eaten in haste during the Exodus, which becomes an annual commemoration. LXX: *arton kakoseos* ("bread of affliction") — literal equivalent.

**Verse 6 — ben ha-arbayim (at the going down of the sun):** "At the going down of the sun" renders *ba-erev ki-vo ha-shemesh* — "in the evening at the coming of the sun." The specification grounds the sacrifice at twilight, the boundary between the 14th and 15th of Abib. LXX: *pros esperan hama dyo tou heliou* — "at evening, at the setting of the sun."

**Verse 7 — uvishalta (you shall cook):** "Cook" for *bishel* (to boil/cook). Deuteronomy's instruction to "cook" (*bishel*) diverges from Exod 12:9 which explicitly says "do not boil" (*al-tivshelu*) but roast (*tsali*). This is a well-known textual tension; the Chronicler (2 Chr 35:13) resolves it by saying they "boiled the Passover" (*vayvashelu*) in fire. LOGOS follows the MT of Deuteronomy precisely. LXX: *hepseseis* — "you shall boil."

**Verses 9–10 — Feast of Weeks / Shavuot:** The Feast of Weeks (*Shavuot*) is dated by counting seven weeks from the beginning of harvest. In later tradition this becomes associated with the giving of the Torah at Sinai. LXX: *heorten hebdomadon* — "Feast of Weeks." The freewill offering (*missat nidvat yadekha*) is proportional to the LORD's blessing.

**Verse 13 — Feast of Booths / Sukkot:** *Chag ha-sukkot* — "Feast of Booths" or "Feast of Tabernacles." The agricultural context is explicit: it follows the gathering of grain and wine. LXX: *heorten skenopegias* ("Feast of Tent-pitching/Tabernacles").

**Verse 16 — yera'eh (shall appear):** "Shall appear before the LORD" for *yera'eh* — a Niphal form that is grammatically passive ("be seen") but functions as an idiom for cultic attendance. The *qere/ketiv* of several manuscripts plays on the ambiguity between "to see" and "to be seen" at the sanctuary. LXX: *ophthesetai* — "shall be seen," preserving the passive.

**Verse 20 — tsedek tsedek tirdof (justice, justice you shall pursue):** The doubled noun *tsedek tsedek* is one of the most celebrated constructions in biblical Hebrew — emphatic repetition expressing intense earnestness. "Justice, justice" retained verbatim. LXX: *dikaion dikaion dioxe* — full reduplication preserved. This principle grounds the appointment of judges (v. 18) and the prohibition on bribes (v. 19) in a positive vision of pursued righteousness.

**Verse 21 — asherah:** "Asherah" retained as a proper noun/cult object name. The Asherah was a wooden pole or sacred tree associated with the Canaanite goddess; it is mentioned alongside *masseboth* (standing stones) throughout Deuteronomy's polemic. LXX: *alsos* ("sacred grove") — a functional translation of the cultic object.

**Verse 22 — massebah (pillar):** "Pillar" for *massebah* — a standing stone. The same word used positively by Jacob at Bethel (Gen 28:18) but here prohibited as associated with Canaanite worship practices. LXX: *stele* — exact equivalent.

*— LOGOS*
